query: recurrence rate in Down’s syndrome due to paternal balanced translocation carriers

retrieved_docs.title: Edwards Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The prevalence of Edwards syndrome is positively correlated with advancing maternal age. The recurrence risk for complete trisomy 18 is 0.5% to 1% for subsequent pregnancies. [4] If 1 parent is found to be a carrier of a balanced translocation leading to an unbalanced translocation in the child, as observed in partial trisomy 18, the recurrence risk can be higher, up to 20%, for subsequent pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: Edwards Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Prenatal counseling for parents with fetuses diagnosed with Edwards syndrome should be comprehensive, including the complexity of the varied presentations at birth, the unavoidable ethical issues due to the high fatality rate, and the significant developmental disability associated with the condition. When a prenatal diagnosis of trisomy 18 is made, the parents have difficulty making decisions regarding resuscitation, life support, intensive care, and surgical options. Clinicians must provide parents with accurate survival figures and comorbidity statistics. The expectant parents should be aware of all available management options during the neonatal period and beyond. Parents benefit by being in touch with family support groups. The parents should be counseled on the recurrence risk of 1% in subsequent pregnancies or as high as 20% in cases of partial trisomy due to unbalanced translocation.

retrieved_docs.title: Jacobsen Syndrome (11q Terminal Deletion Syndrome) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Prenatal counseling requirements can differ depending on the specific genetic rearrangement involved. Generally, de novo genetic variants, which arise spontaneously rather than being inherited, are not passed down from parents to offspring. However, in cases of gonadal mosaicism, where genetic variations are present in reproductive cells but not in the parents' somatic cells, siblings may exhibit the same variants despite no physical traits in the parents. If a parent is affected by a genetic condition, the recurrence risk for future pregnancies is 50%, and prenatal counseling is essential to address individual genetic risks.

retrieved_docs.title: Patau Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Patau syndrome is diagnosed either prenatally or at birth. [1] Advanced maternal age is a risk for trisomy 13 due to an increased frequency of nondisjunction. [1] However, 20% of Patau syndrome can result from an unbalanced translocation and rarely by mosaicism. [1] [3] Multiple large studies have detailed the poor prognosis of patients with Patau syndrome. Median survival is 7 to 10 days in live-born patients, and 90% live for less than 1 year. [1] [4] Recently, there have been reports of several cases of longer survival due to aggressive medical therapy. [4] Long surviving patients with Patau syndrome are less likely to have cerebral and cardiovascular malformations, typically the primary cause of the poor prognosis with Patau syndrome. [1] [4] Even in these cases of increased survival, severe disability remains the expectation of these patients. [4]

retrieved_docs.content: In couples with chromosomal abnormalities, the first step is a referral to genetic counseling. Couples should be educated on the potential likelihood of having fetal chromosomal abnormalities in future pregnancies. They may choose to proceed with

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of Down syndrome increases with maternal age, and its occurrence varies in different populations (1 in 319 to 1 in 1000 live births) [7] [8] . It is also known that the frequency of Down syndrome fetuses is quite high at the time of conception, but about 50% to 75% of these fetuses are lost before term. The occurrence of other autosomal trisomy is much more common than the 21, but the postnatal survival is very poor as compared to Down syndrome. This high percentage of survival of patients with trisomy 21 is thought to be a function of a small number of genes on chromosome 21 called Hsa21, which is the smallest and least dense of the autosomes. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Chromosome Abnormalities -- Clinical Significance -- Trisomy 21 (Down Syndrome)
retrieved_docs.content: Incidence: Approximately 1:700 to 1/800 live births (most common autosomal trisomy) Karyotype: 47, XX or XY, +21 Clinical features: Characteristic facial appearance, flat facies, prominent epicanthic folds, flat occiput, Brushfiel spot in irides, weak muscle tone at birth, single transverse palmar crease (simian crease), clinodactyly, congenital digestive and cardiac defects, intellectual disability, increased risk for leukemia, Alzheimer disease and hearing an vision problems Prognosis: Approximately 75% of conceptions die in embryonic or fetal life. Nevertheless, if birth occurs, the prognosis is relatively good (median survival is 47 years). It is the most common aneuploidy that is compatible with long-term survival. Note: Approximately 95% of patients with Down syndrome have trisomy 21. However, 4% of cases are due to Robertsonian translocation, and 1% is due to mosaicism. [4] [6] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Pregnancy Loss -- Treatment / Management -- Chromosomal Anomalies
retrieved_docs.content: prenatal genetic testing, such as preimplantation genetic diagnosis, chorionic villus sampling, or amniocentesis to identify genetic anomalies in the fetus and decide about further treatment options. [16] Although embryos with unbalanced chromosomal arrangements can theoretically be screened out, PGT (preimplantation genetic testing) is not routinely advised since the likelihood of a pregnancy with an unbalanced karyotype surviving into the second trimester is low. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Recurrent Pregnancy Loss -- Evaluation -- Genetic Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Karyotype assessment of the couples has to be offered to recognize underlying balanced, reciprocal, or Robertsonian translocations or mosaicism that might be transmitted to the fetus, causing RPL. Though these tests are of low yield and expensive, one should consider evaluating the karyotypes of the couples with RPL. [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetics, Chromosome Abnormalities -- Cellular
retrieved_docs.content: Regarding structural chromosomal abnormalities, there is one major mechanism of significant clinical importance called Robertsonian translocation. In this case, two acrocentric chromosomes break at the centromere, losing both of their short p arms and creating a new single chromosome containing one centromere and the q arms of both original chromosomes. Some individuals may have this kind of cytogenic aberration and appear phenotypically normal because only small amounts of genetic material are present in the short arms of acrocentric chromosomes. Thus, the two remaining long arms can compensate for this loss. However, in some cases, it can lead to genetic imbalances and the formation of zygotes with polyploidies (trisomies or monosomies) with significant phenotypical consequences (i.e., Down syndrome). The estimated carrier frequency for Robertsonian translocations in the general population is 1 in 1000. [4] [6]

retrieved_docs.title: CHARGE Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: CHARGE syndrome is a congenital disorder with an incidence of approximately 1 in 10000 births. It does not have sex-linked expression, so both males and females are equally affected. Most cases are sporadic and not inherited, but mean paternal age appears to be slightly increased. In 2010, a study on 379 patients diagnosed with CHARGE syndrome was conducted, followed by CHD7 mutation testing, and the result proved that 67% of cases were due to a CHD7 mutation. [4] There is a low recurrence rate of 1%.

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Cell-free fetal DNA makes up 5% to 10% of the maternal plasma, and it increases during pregnancy and clears after delivery. Though this method has been used to determine fetal Rh status in Rhive women [51] , sex in sex-linked disorders [52] , and for the detection of paternally inherited autosomal recessive and dominant traits, [53] its use for the detection of chromosomal aneuploidy, especially the trisomy is still a challenge. Few other recent methods like digital PCR and next-generation sequencing (NGS) are also being developed for the diagnosis of Down syndrome. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Trisomy 13 -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Trisomy 13 is the third most common nondisjunction meiotic triploidy followed by Edwards and Down syndrome. [1] The three genetic presentations are complete nondisjunction trisomy 13, a Robertsonian translocation, and mosaicism. The most common cause of holoprosencephaly is related to trisomy 13. [1] During the first-trimester screening of trisomy 13, FNT will appear equal or greater than 3.5mm, with a decreased B-hCG, and PAPP-A. [11] Most gestations with trisomy 13 are terminated, the continuation of pregnancy increases the risk of preeclampsia. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Trisomy 13 -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Trisomy 13 arises from the nondisjunction of germ cells during meiosis I or II of either parental cells. [5] Nonetheless, maternal germ cell nondisjunction correlated to the increased age of conception contributes to 91% of cases. [3] The mode of inheritance for the complete trisomy 13 is caused by spontaneous interference in meiosis, while vertical inheritance is hereditary in balanced translocations. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Down Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: An extra copy of chromosome 21 is associated with Down syndrome, which occurs due to the failure of chromosome 21 to separate during gametogenesis, resulting in an extra chromosome in all the body cells. Robertsonian translocation and isochromosome or ring chromosome are the other 2 possible causes of trisomy 21. Isochromosome is a condition when 2 long arms separate together instead of the long and short arms while in Robertsonian translocation. This occurs in 2% to 4% of the patients. The long arm of chromosome 21 is attached to another chromosome, mostly chromosome 14. In mosaicism, there are 2 different cell lines because of the error of division after fertilization. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Trisomy 13 -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology and epidemiology of trisomy 13. Review the factors relating to the prenatal diagnosis of trisomy 13. Summarize the management options available for trisomy 13. Describe some interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication in the diagnosis of trisomy 13. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2811
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are clinically associated with إنهاء الحمل – اختياري (optional termination of pregnancy) and also have a relationship with اضطراب رحمي (uterine disorder). This indicates that uterine disorders are considered among the other complications of pregnancy and that optional termination of pregnancy can be clinically linked to these pregnancy‑related complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
retrieved_docs.content: CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY and SIZE DATES are both clinically associated with إجهاض تلقائي (spontaneous abortion). In turn, إجهاض تلقائي is linked as a precursor condition to إجهاض معتاد (recurrent abortion), indicating that a history of spontaneous miscarriage can lead to repeated pregnancy loss. Additionally, dydrogesteron is reported to may prevent إجهاض تلقائي, suggesting a potential therapeutic role for this progestogen in reducing the risk of spontaneous miscarriage.

retrieved_docs.content: Databases are clinically associated with نواتج متبقية من الحمل and with gestation twin, indicating that medical information systems are linked to the monitoring and analysis of residual pregnancy outcomes as well as twin gestations, thereby supporting clinical decision‑making and research in obstetrics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2007
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnancy observable is employed as an interpretive tool for إجهاض معتاد, indicating that observable signs and findings during pregnancy can be used to recognize or assess the condition of recurrent miscarriage. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone are both hormonal agents that may help prevent إجهاض تلقائي (spontaneous abortion). In contrast, a history of CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY is clinically associated with an increased likelihood of إجهاض تلقائي, indicating that prior fetal structural anomalies can be a risk factor for subsequent spontaneous loss. Understanding that 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone can be used prophylactically, while recognizing the heightened risk linked to previous congenital malformations, is important for optimizing patient counseling and management strategies aimed at reducing the incidence of spontaneous abortion.

Maternal pregnancy complications as a whole are nested within a larger, unclassified group of maternal complications (مضاعفات الحمل التي تصيب الأم، غير مصنف في موضع آخر), emphasizing that many of these disorders are interrelated and may require coordinated assessment, risk stratification, and management across the ante‑partum, intrapartum, and post‑partum periods.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Poor reproductive history and elective termination of pregnancy are clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This relationship indicates that women who have experienced a suboptimal reproductive history or who have undergone an elective termination of pregnancy may be at increased risk for developing preterm labor. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians identify patients who require closer monitoring and targeted interventions to prevent or manage preterm labor, thereby improving maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2707
retrieved_docs.content: خديج is clinically associated with cervical insufficiency (قصور عنق الرحم), indicating a direct link between this condition and weakened cervical competence. Additionally, recurrent miscarriage (إجهاض معتاد) is identified as a parent condition of cervical insufficiency, suggesting that women who experience repeated pregnancy loss may have an underlying cervical insufficiency contributing to the miscarriages. Recognizing both the association of خديج with cervical insufficiency and the role of إجهاض معتاد as a precursor condition is important for accurate diagnosis and targeted management of patients at risk for cervical incompetence.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1697
retrieved_docs.content: A caesarean section scar of unknown type and a poor reproductive history are clinically associated with مخاض قبل الأوان (preterm labor), indicating that women who have an indeterminate type of previous cesarean scar or who have experienced suboptimal reproductive outcomes are at increased risk for premature labor. Recognizing these associations enables clinicians to identify high‑risk patients, implement closer surveillance, and consider preventive strategies to reduce the likelihood of preterm delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.
